Docker Commands
Some of the most commonly used docker commands are 
 docker images
Lists docker images on the host machine.
 docker build
Builds image from Dockerfile.
 docker run
Runs a Docker container. 
There are many arguments which you can pass to this command for example,
 docker run -d - Run container in background and print container ID
 docker run -p - Port mapping
use docker run --help to look into more arguments.
 docker ps
Lists running containers on the host machine.
 docker stop
Stops running container.
 docker start
Starts a stopped container.
 docker rm
Removes a stopped container.
 docker rmi
Removes an image from the host machine.
 docker pull
Downloads an image from the configured registry.
 docker push
Uploads an image to the configured registry.
 docker exec
Run a command in a running container.
 docker network
Manage Docker networks such as creating and removing networks, and connecting containers to networks.

Docker Networking
Networking allows containers to communicate with each other and with the host system. Containers run isolated from the host system
and need a way to communicate with each other and with the host system.
By default, Docker provides two network drivers for you, the bridge and the overlay drivers. 
 
docker network ls
 
 
NETWORK ID NAME DRIVER
xxxxxxxxxxxx none null
xxxxxxxxxxxx host host
xxxxxxxxxxxx bridge bridge
 
 Bridge Networking
The default network mode in Docker. It creates a private network between the host and containers, allowing
containers to communicate with each other and with the host system.
! image 
If you want to secure your containers and isolate them from the default bridge network you can also create your own bridge network.
 
docker network create -d bridge my_bridge
 
Now, if you list the docker networks, you will see a new network.
 
docker network ls
NETWORK ID NAME DRIVER
xxxxxxxxxxxx bridge bridge
xxxxxxxxxxxx my_bridge bridge

Now, if you list the docker networks, you will see a new network.
 
docker network ls
NETWORK ID NAME DRIVER
xxxxxxxxxxxx bridge bridge
xxxxxxxxxxxx my_bridge bridge
xxxxxxxxxxxx none null
xxxxxxxxxxxx host host
 
This new network can be attached to the containers, when you run these containers.
 
docker run -d --net my_bridge --name db training postgres
 
This way, you can run multiple containers on a single host platform where one container is attached to the default network and 
the other is attached to the my_bridge network.
These containers are completely isolated with their private networks and cannot talk to each other.
! image 
However, you can at any point of time, attach the first container to my_bridge network and enable communication
 
docker network connect my_bridge web
 
! image 
 Host Networking
This mode allows containers to share the host system's network stack, providing direct access to the host system's network.

docker network connect my_bridge web
 
! image 
 Host Networking
This mode allows containers to share the host system's network stack, providing direct access to the host system's network.
To attach a host network to a Docker container, you can use the --network "host" option when running a docker run command. When you use this option, the container has access to the host's network stack, and shares the host's network namespace. This means that the container will use the same IP address and network configuration as the host.
Here's an example of how to run a Docker container with the host network:
 
docker run --network "host" image_name command 
 
Keep in mind that when you use the host network, the container is less isolated from the host system, and has access to all of the host's network resources. This can be a security risk, so use the host network with caution.

Additionally, not all Docker image and command combinations are compatible with the host network, so it's important to check the image documentation or run the image with the --network "bridge" option the default network mode first to see if there are any compatibility issues.
 Overlay Networking
This mode enables communication between containers across multiple Docker host machines, allowing containers to be connected to a single network even when they are running on different hosts.
 Macvlan Networking
This mode allows a container to appear on the network as a physical host rather than as a container.

Docker Volumes
 Problem Statement
It is a very common requirement to persist the data in a Docker container beyond the lifetime of the container. However, the file system
of a Docker container is deleted removed when the container dies. 
 Solution
There are 2 different ways how docker solves this problem.
1. Volumes
2. Bind Directory on a host as a Mount
 Volumes 
Volumes aims to solve the same problem by providing a way to store data on the host file system, separate from the container's file system, 
so that the data can persist even if the container is deleted and recreated.
! image 
Volumes can be created and managed using the docker volume command. You can create a new volume using the following command:
 
docker volume create volume_name 
 
Once a volume is created, you can mount it to a container using the -v or --mount option when running a docker run command. 
For example:
 
docker run -it -v volume_name : data image_name bin bash

Once a volume is created, you can mount it to a container using the -v or --mount option when running a docker run command. 
For example:
 
docker run -it -v volume_name : data image_name bin bash
 
This command will mount the volume volume_name to the data directory in the container. Any data written to the data directory
inside the container will be persisted in the volume on the host file system.
 Bind Directory on a host as a Mount
Bind mounts also aims to solve the same problem but in a complete different way.
Using this way, user can mount a directory from the host file system into a container. Bind mounts have the same behavior as volumes, but
are specified using a host path instead of a volume name. 
For example, 
 
docker run -it -v host_path : container_path image_name bin bash
 
 Key Differences between Volumes and Bind Directory on a host as a Mount

For example, 
 
docker run -it -v host_path : container_path image_name bin bash
 
 Key Differences between Volumes and Bind Directory on a host as a Mount
Volumes are managed, created, mounted and deleted using the Docker API. However, Volumes are more flexible than bind mounts, as 
they can be managed and backed up separately from the host file system, and can be moved between containers and hosts.
In a nutshell, Bind Directory on a host as a Mount are appropriate for simple use cases where you need to mount a directory from the host file system into
a container, while volumes are better suited for more complex use cases where you need more control over the data being persisted
in the container.

1. DATA VERSION CONTROL 
In the era of Big Data and artificial intelligence, data has become the most valuable asset for any 
organization. It fuels machine learning ML models, forms the foundation for business strategies, and 
provides the evidence that underpins scientific resea rch. However, unlike other digital assets such as 
source code, data is fluid, complex, and often chaotic. It changes, grows, and evolves at a breakneck 
pace, presenting a fundamental challenge: how can we manage this evolution reliably, reproducibly, 
and collaboratively? 
The answer lies in Data Version Control DVC , an emerging discipline that adapts software 
management principles to the data ecosystem. It's not simply about making backups or naming files 
with dates. It's a systematic approach to recording every significant change to a dataset, enabling teams

with dates. It's a systematic approach to recording every significant change to a dataset, enabling teams 
to understand its history, revert to previous states, and collaborate seamlessly. This section lays the 
conceptual groundwork for DVC, explores the critical problems it solves, and defines the essential 
terminology every data professional should know. 
1.1. Fundamentals of data versioning 
To understand the need for data version control, it's helpful to look at how more established disciplines 
have solved similar problems. Software engineering, for example, would have been unthinkable 
without tools like Git, which allow thousands of developers to collaborate on massive codebases. Data 
versioning draws inspiration from this revolution but adapts to the unique challenges presented by 
data. 
1.1.1. History and evolution 
The concept of version control isn't entirely new, but its formalization and the creation of specialized

data. 
1.1.1. History and evolution 
The concept of version control isn't entirely new, but its formalization and the creation of specialized 
tools are relatively recent. Historically, data management was done manually and in a rudimentary 
way, an approach that quickly became unsustainable. 
 Phase 1: Manual versioning The era of organized chaos .The most basic and, unfortunately, still 
common method. It consists of saving multiple copies of a file with descriptive names: 
 sales_report_2023_final.csv 
 sales_report_2023_final_corrected.csv 
 sales_report_2023_final_final_v2_NOW_YES.csv 
This strategy is error -prone, confusing, and doesn't scale. There's no central record of changes, 
it's impossible to tell what differentiates '_fixed' from '_v2', and collaboration is a recipe for 
disaster, with multiple "final versions" circulating via email. 
 Phase 2: Centralized storage with backups. An improvement over the manual method. Data is

disaster, with multiple "final versions" circulating via email. 
 Phase 2: Centralized storage with backups. An improvement over the manual method. Data is 
stored on a central server or in the cloud e.g., SharePoint, Google Drive , and regular backups are 
performed. This protects against data loss, but it's not a true version control system. Reverting to 
a previous version involves restoring a full backup, a slow and disruptive process. Furthermore, it 
doesn't record the reasons behind changes or allow for working on parallel branches or 
experiments. 
 Phase 3: Adapting coding tools the brave attempt . With the rise of data science, teams tried

Technical guide Data version control 7 
 
 
 
directly applying tools like Git to datasets. They soon discovered its limitations. Git is optimized 
for small text files and lines of code. When faced with a 10GB CSV file, its performance degrades 
dramatically. Storing every version of a large dataset in Git would cause the repository to grow to 
an unmanageable size. Tools like Git LFS Large File Storage emerged as a workaround, storing 
pointers in Git and the large files elsewhere, but they still failed to address data granularity and 
lineage. 
 Phase 4: Native data version control tools. The obvious need led to the development of 
specialized tools like DVC Data Version Control , Pachyderm, and Delta Lake. These solutions are 
designed from the ground up for data. They understand that data is large and often binary. Instead 
of simply copyin g data within the repository, they manage metadata and pointers, integrating

designed from the ground up for data. They understand that data is large and often binary. Instead 
of simply copyin g data within the repository, they manage metadata and pointers, integrating 
with optimized storage systems like Amazon S3 and Google Cloud Storage and leveraging Git for 
what it does best: versioning code and metadata. 
1.1.2. Fundamental differences with code versioning 
The reason Git doesn't work well for "unsupported" data is that code and data are fundamentally 
different. Thinking of them as the same thing is the root of many problems in data projects. Consider 
that versioning code is like editing a book manuscript. Ch anges are textual, incremental, and easy to 
compare diff . Versioning data is like managing versions of a high-definition movie. The file is massive, 
opaque binary , and a small change correcting a single frame creates a completely new file from a 
storage perspective. You don't compare "lines" of film; you compare the final result.

opaque binary , and a small change correcting a single frame creates a completely new file from a 
storage perspective. You don't compare "lines" of film; you compare the final result.

1.1.3. Unique characteristics of data as a digital asset 
Data is not just "big files". It has intrinsic characteristics that demand a specialized management 
approach. 
1. Data Gravity:It's a term coined to describe the idea that as data accumulates in size, it tends to 
attract more applications and services. Moving a 100 TB dataset is expensive, slow, and risky. 
Therefore, it's easier to move the processing code to where the data resi des. CVD systems

attract more applications and services. Moving a 100 TB dataset is expensive, slow, and risky. 
Therefore, it's easier to move the processing code to where the data resi des. CVD systems 
respect this principle by keeping the data in a centralized, optimized storage and moving only the 
lightweight metadata files. 
2. Data Entropy:Data systems naturally tend toward disorder. Without active governance, schemas 
change, quality degrades, and documentation becomes outdated. A CVD system acts as a force 
against entropy, enforcing structure, recording metadata, and ensuring that each version is well-
defined and recoverable. 
3. Complex dependencies:A single end result, such as a BI report or an ML model, can depend on a 
complex processing chain: raw data from an API, a cleaning script, an enrichment process that 
queries a database, and finally a training script. CVD not only versions the final file, but ideally

complex processing chain: raw data from an API, a cleaning script, an enrichment process that 
queries a database, and finally a training script. CVD not only versions the final file, but ideally 
captures this entire dependency pipeline, allowing the entire chain to be reproduced from 
beginning to end. 
1.2. Current problems in data projects 
The absence of a robust data version control system is not merely a technical inconvenience; it is a 
constant source of risk, inefficiency, and failure in data projects. When the question "What data was 
used to obtain this result?" cannot be answered with certainty, the entire foundation of the project 
becomes unstable. 
1.2.1. Detailed problem cases: three real-world scenarios 
These scenarios, based on real anonymized problems, illustrate the tangible consequences of poor 
version control. 
Scenario 1: The Ghost Model 
 Situation:A data science team at an e -commerce company develops a product recommendation

version control. 
Scenario 1: The Ghost Model 
 Situation:A data science team at an e -commerce company develops a product recommendation 
model. After months of work, they achieve 92 accuracy in their tests and deploy it to production. 
For the first few weeks, it works perfectly, increasing sales by 15 . 
 The problem: Two months later, the model's performance plummets. Recommendations are

Technical guide Data version control 10 
 
 
 
irrelevant, and the click-through rate collapses. The team decides to retrain the model to adapt it 
to the new customer data. However, the original data scientist has left the company. Their 
successor finds the training script on Git, but the training_data .csv file on the shared server has 
been updated several times since the initial deployment with new marketing campaigns and data 
corrections. There is no record of which exact version was used for the original, successful model. 
 Consequences:The team spends weeks trying to "guess" the original state of the data, testing 
different filters and preprocessing methods without success. They are unable to replicate the 92 
accuracy. The company loses the competitive advantage of a 15 increase in sal es, and trust in 
the data team erodes. 
 Solution with CVD:With a CVD system, the Git commit containing the final version of the training

the data team erodes. 
 Solution with CVD:With a CVD system, the Git commit containing the final version of the training 
script would have been linked to a specific hash of the data version. The new data scientist would 
simply have to run dvc pull to download the exact version of the data from two months ago 
and reproduce the original training environment in minutes, allowing them to analyze what had 
changed and why performance had degraded model drift . 
Scenario 2: The impossible audit 
 Situation:A financial institution must submit quarterly regulatory reports on its risk exposure. 
These reports are generated using complex models that are fed with real-time market data stock 
prices, interest rates, etc. . 
 The problem:One year after submitting the Q2 report, regulators requested an audit and asked 
the entity to reproduce the exact figures from that specific report and justify its calculations. The

The problem:One year after submitting the Q2 report, regulators requested an audit and asked 
the entity to reproduce the exact figures from that specific report and justify its calculations. The 
team has the risk model code from that period. However, the market databa se has been 
continuously updated. The Q2 data from the previous year has been corrected by the vendor, and 
some time series have been adjusted. Recreating the exact state of the database at that time is a 
monumental, if not impossible, task. 
 Consequences:The IT team spends weeks trying to restore backups and cross -reference logs to 
reconstruct the state of the data. The result is not 100 identical. The organization faces potential 
penalties for failing to guarantee the traceability of its reports, and its reputation suffers a severe 
blow. 
 Solution with CVD:A workflow using CVD would take an immutable snapshot of the market data

blow. 
 Solution with CVD:A workflow using CVD would take an immutable snapshot of the market data 
used for each quarterly report. This snapshot would be versioned and associated with the report's 
code. When an audit arrived, the team would simply retrieve the report_risk_Q2_2024 v ersion 
from their project, and the system would automatically download the corresponding market data 
snapshot, allowing the report to be regenerated with 100 accuracy within hours. 
Scenario 3: Collaborative Chaos 
 Situation:Two data analysts, Ana and Bruno, are working on the same project to segment 
customers. They both start with a common dataset: customers_raw.csv. 
 The problem:Ana decides that customers without a last purchase date should be removed. She 
creates a script, generates customers_cleaned_ana.csv, and begins her analysis. Simultaneously, 
Bruno decides that customers without a last purchase date should be imputed using the mean.

Technical guide Data version control 11 
 
 
 
He creates his own script, generates customers_cleaned_bruno.csv, and continues his work. A 
week later, a third team member, Carla, needs the clean dataset and finds both files. She doesn't 
know which one is the "official" one, what criteria were used in each, or if she can combine them. 
The situation worsens when Ana updates her file, accidentally overwriting Bruno's file in a shared 
folder. 
 Consequences:Hours of work are lost. There is confusion and frustration within the team. Ana's 
and Bruno's analyses are not comparable because they are based on different cleaning 
assumptions. The lack of a single "source of truth" breeds distrust in the data. 
 Solution with CVD: Ana and Bruno would create two data "branches," just like in Git. The main 
branch would contain the original dataset. Ana would create a branch called feature remove-null-

Solution with CVD: Ana and Bruno would create two data "branches," just like in Git. The main 
branch would contain the original dataset. Ana would create a branch called feature remove-null-
customers and Bruno one called feature input-null-customers. Each would work on their isolated 
version. Their changes and the logic behind them would be documented in commit messages. 
Later, the team could review both strategies, compare them, and decide which one or a 
combination to incorporate into the main branch as the official, clean version of the dataset. 
1.2.2. Costs of not having version control 
The costs arising from these problems are not only technical, but also directly impact the business and 
mission of the organization. 
 Financial costs: 
 Lost work hours: Data scientists and engineers, whose salaries are high, spend a 
disproportionate amount of time on "data archaeology" instead of generating value.

Financial costs: 
 Lost work hours: Data scientists and engineers, whose salaries are high, spend a 
disproportionate amount of time on "data archaeology" instead of generating value. 
 Faulty business decisions:Basing strategies on incorrect analysis or flawed models can lead 
to losses of millions. 
 Regulatory fines: The inability to demonstrate traceability in regulated sectors finance, 
health can lead to severe penalties. 
 Opportunity costs: 
 Slowing down of innovation:If each new project involves reinventing the wheel to clean and 
validate the data, the pace of innovation slows down. 
 Inefficient collaboration: Teams become afraid to touch the data for fear of breaking 
something, creating silos and duplication of effort. 
 Reputation costs: 
 Loss of trust:both internal from management in the data team and external from clients or 
the scientific community . 
 Damage to the brand:A public error due to incorrect data can have a lasting impact on brand 
perception.

the scientific community . 
 Damage to the brand:A public error due to incorrect data can have a lasting impact on brand 
perception. 
1.2.3. Impact on reproducibility and collaboration 
 Scientific reproducibility:In science, reproducibility is the cornerstone of validity. An experiment 
that cannot be replicated by others is unreliable. In data science, this means that an analysis or 
model must be able to be exactly regenerated, yielding the same result. Without versioning the 
data, reproducibility is simply a utopia. 
 Team collaboration:Modern teamwork is distributed and asynchronous. CVD provides a common

Technical guide Data version control 12 
 
 
 
language and workflow. It allows team members to work in parallel, experiment with data safely 
in isolated branches, and merge their contributions in an orderly fashion, much like software 
developers collaborate on complex codebases. Without it, collaboration is limited to sending files 
via email, a practice that inevitably leads to chaos. 
1.3. Fundamental concepts and terminology 
To effectively implement and use a CVD system, understanding its vocabulary is crucial. These concepts 
form the grammar of data versioning. 
1.3.1. Snapshots 
A snapshot is a read -only, completely immutable copy of the state of a dataset at a specific point in 
time. It is the most fundamental concept of CVD. 
 Analogy:Think of a document's "Save As..." function, but with superpowers. When you create a 
snapshot, you're not simply saving a copy. You're creating a permanent, identifiable version that

Analogy:Think of a document's "Save As..." function, but with superpowers. When you create a 
snapshot, you're not simply saving a copy. You're creating a permanent, identifiable version that 
can't be altered. Any future changes will require creating a new snapshot. 
 Practical example: 
1. A team receives the January sales dataset: sales_january.csv. 
2. They decide to version it. Using a CVD tool, they create the first snapshot. The tool calculates 
a unique "digital fingerprint" a checksum for the file and stores it in a secure location e.g., 
an S3 bucket . In their Git repository, only a small metadat a file pointing to that snapshot is 
stored. 
3. Later, they discover errors in some transactions. They correct the file. 
4. Now, they create a second snapshot. The tool detects that the file has changed, calculates a 
new checksum, uploads the new version to storage, and creates a new metadata file.

4. Now, they create a second snapshot. The tool detects that the file has changed, calculates a 
new checksum, uploads the new version to storage, and creates a new metadata file. 
5. They now have two immutable and identifiable versions of the January dataset. They can 
switch between them with a single command, knowing that version_1 will always be the 
original file and version_2 will always be the corrected file. 
1.3.2. Data Lineage 
Data lineage is the complete record of the data lifecycle. It documents its origin, every transformation 
it has undergone, and where it has moved over time. 
 Analogy:It is the family tree of a piece of data. It shows who its "parents" were raw data sources , 
how it was "educated" cleaning and transformation scripts , and what it "became" final dataset, 
model, or report . 
 Practical example:A good CVD system not only versions model_churn.pkl, but also records the

model, or report . 
 Practical example:A good CVD system not only versions model_churn.pkl, but also records the 
entire preprocessing, training, and testing graph. This means that if someone questions the model, 
it can be traced back to see that it was generated with train_model.py, which in tu rn used 
sales_clean.parquet, which came from two original sources processed by clean_sales.py. If 
sales_clean.parquet changes, the system knows that model_churn.pkl is outdated and needs to 
be regenerated.

Technical guide Data version control 13 
 
 
 
1.3.3. Metadata Tracking 
Metadata is "data about data." Metadata tracking involves recording crucial contextual information 
about each version of the dataset. 
 Use case:Imagine two versions of a model: model_v1 has 85 accuracy and model_v2 has 88 
accuracy. Without metadata, we don't know why v2 is better. With metadata tracking, we can 
record: 
 Version metadata:who created it, when, and with what commit message "New marketing 
campaign data added" . 
 Schema metadata:the number of columns, their names, and data types. Perhaps v2 used a 
new column feature that improved performance. 
 Descriptive metadata:statistics such as the number of rows, the average of a key column, the 
number of null values. 
 Evaluation metrics:accuracy, F1-score, or any other metric relevant to the use case. 
By comparing this metadata, the team can quickly understand that v2 is better because it was 
trained on more data and used a new informative feature.

By comparing this metadata, the team can quickly understand that v2 is better because it was 
trained on more data and used a new informative feature. 
1.3.4. Checksums and data integrity 
A checksum is a fixed-length alphanumeric value that acts as a unique digital fingerprint for a file. It is 
calculated using a cryptographic algorithm. If even a single bit changes in the original file, the checksum 
will change drastically. 
Practical example: 
1. We versioned the clients.csv file. The CVD tool calculates its hash: 
f1d2d2f924e986ac86fdf7b36c94bcdf. 
2. This hash is stored in the metadata file. 
3. When another team member downloads this version, the tool recalculates the hash of the 
downloaded file. 
4. If the hash matches f1d2d2f924e986ac86fdf7b36c94bcdf, there is mathematical certainty 
that the file is identical to the original, with no corruption during transfer or storage. If it does

4. If the hash matches f1d2d2f924e986ac86fdf7b36c94bcdf, there is mathematical certainty 
that the file is identical to the original, with no corruption during transfer or storage. If it does 
not match, the tool will alert you to an error, protecting against the use of corrupted data. 
1.3.5. DataProvenance vs. Data Lineage 
Although often used interchangeably, these terms have nuances. 
 Provenance:It focuses on the origin and history of a piece of data. It answers the question: "Where 
did this data come from, and who has accessed it?" It's a concept more closely related to auditing 
and trust. For example, the provenance of a medical image could incl ude the scanner that 
captured it, the technician who operated it, and the date. 
 Lineage:It focuses on flow and transformation. It answers the question: "What technical steps led 
to the creation of this data?" It's a concept closer to reproducibility and pipeline debugging.

Lineage:It focuses on flow and transformation. It answers the question: "What technical steps led 
to the creation of this data?" It's a concept closer to reproducibility and pipeline debugging. 
In summary: provenance is the "who" and "where" of the data history, while lineage is the technical 
"how" and "why." A robust CVD system must capture both.

Technical guide Data version control 14 
 
 
 
1.4. Critical application cases 
The need for data version control is not abstract; it manifests itself in critical applications where 
reliability and traceability are non-negotiable. 
1.4.1. Machine Learning models in production 
A production ML model is a living system. It's not enough to simply deploy it and forget about it. It 
requires constant monitoring and management, where CVD is essential. 
Detailed explanation: 
A model is the result of three components: code the algorithm , data used for training , and 
parameters the training configuration . To reproduce a model, all three are needed. Git manages the 
code. The CVD manages the data. Configuration files also versioned in Git manage the parameters. 
 Model drift management: Models degrade over time because the real world changes 
 concept drift . When the performance of a production model declines, the first step is to

Model drift management: Models degrade over time because the real world changes 
 concept drift . When the performance of a production model declines, the first step is to 
compare it to previous versions. Is the current model worse than the one from three months 
ago with the same data? Or is it the new data that's different? CVD allows you to perform 
these experiments, isolating the cause of the problem. 
 Rollbacksinsurance:If a newly deployed model turns out to be faulty, it's crucial to be able to 
quickly and safely revert to the previous version. A rollback involves not only changing the 
model file but also ensuring that all the infrastructure that consumes it e.g., infer ence 
pipelines is compatible. CVD, by versioning the model as an artifact along with its lineage, 
facilitates this process. 
 Comparison of experiments:A data scientist can train dozens of models by varying the input

facilitates this process. 
 Comparison of experiments:A data scientist can train dozens of models by varying the input 
data e.g., with or without an external data source . CVD allows each of these experiments to 
be recorded, associating each resulting model with the exact data that created it and its 
performance metrics, facilitating rigorous comparison and the selection of the best candidate 
for production. 
1.4.2. Regulatory audits 
In sectors such as finance, pharmaceuticals, and any industry subject to regulations such as GDPR, the 
ability to demonstrate where data comes from and how it has been used is not optional; it is a legal 
requirement. 
Specific examples: 
 GDPR General Data Protection Regulation :If a European citizen exercises their "right to be 
forgotten," the company must be able to demonstrate that it has removed their data from all 
systems. A clear data lineage is essential to trace where that customer's data has flowed and 
ensure its complete deletion.

systems. A clear data lineage is essential to trace where that customer's data has flowed and 
ensure its complete deletion. 
 Financial sector:A bank must be able to explain to regulators how the value at risk VaR of 
its portfolio was calculated on a specific date. This requires the model code, the parameters, 
and, crucially, the exact snapshot of market data from that day. 
 Clinical research:When submitting a new drug for approval, pharmaceutical companies must

Technical guide Data version control 15 
 
 
 
provide a comprehensive record of clinical trial data. Every analysis and conclusion must be 
traceable back to the original raw data, ensuring that there has been no manipulation. 
1.4.3. Reproducible scientific research 
The reproducibility crisis is a serious problem in many scientific fields. CVD is one of the most powerful 
tools to combat it. It allows you to package the code, data, and instructions for running an analysis into 
a single project, creating an "executable research object." This means that peer reviewers can go 
beyond simply reading the article; with just a few commands, they can regenerate the results, 
validating the robustness of the work. 
1.4.4. Collaboration in distributed teams 
In today's global work environment, it's rare for a data team to be located in the same office. Members 
may be in different cities or even continents. The CVD provides the necessary infrastructure for

may be in different cities or even continents. The CVD provides the necessary infrastructure for 
efficient, asynchronous collaboration. It acts as a cen tralized "single source of truth." An analyst in 
Madrid can process the data and create a new version. A machine learning engineer in Bangalore can 
take that version, train a model, and record it. The entire change history is transparent, preventing 
conflicts and ensuring everyone is working with the correct information. 
2. VERSIONING STRATEGIES AND METHODOLOGIES 
Once the fundamentals of Data Version Control DVC have been established and its critical importance 
understood, the next step is to explore practical strategies and methodologies for its implementation. 
This section delves into the "how": 
 How to name versions consistently. 
 How to efficiently store changes. 
 How to manage the contextual information that gives meaning to each version.

This section delves into the "how": 
 How to name versions consistently. 
 How to efficiently store changes. 
 How to manage the contextual information that gives meaning to each version. 
 How to adapt collaborative software workflows, such as branches and merges, to the world of 
data. 
Adopting a structured approach not only brings technical clarity but also establishes a common 
language for the team, enabling more robust data governance and smoother collaboration. Decisions 
made at this stage will have a direct impact on storage costs, team agility, and the organization's ability 
to audit and reproduce its data assets. 
2.1. Semantic versioning adapted to data 
Semantic versioning SemVer is a widely adopted standard in software development for 
communicating the nature of changes between versions. Its MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH format provides a 
clear signal as to whether a new version introduces changes that break compa tibility. Adapting this

communicating the nature of changes between versions. Its MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH format provides a 
clear signal as to whether a new version introduces changes that break compa tibility. Adapting this 
logic to datasets provides a predictable and powerful framework for managing their evolution.

Technical guide Data version control 16 
 
 
 
2.1.1. Detailed definition of MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH for datasets 
In the context of data, "compatibility" refers to whether a pipeline or script that consumed the previous 
version can run without errors with the new version. 
 MAJOR Major change - breaks compatibility :This number increases when changes are made to 
the dataset schema that are incompatible with the previous version. Code or analysis that worked 
with version 1.xx will likely fail or produce incorrect results if run on version 2.0.0 without 
modifications. These are deep structural changes. 
 MINOR Minor Change - adds functionality without breaking : It increments when new data or 
features columns are added in a way that doesn't break backward compatibility. A script that 
consumed version 1.1.x should be able to process version 1.2.0 without problems, although it 
might ignore the newly added columns or rows. The changes are additive.

consumed version 1.1.x should be able to process version 1.2.0 without problems, although it 
might ignore the newly added columns or rows. The changes are additive. 
 PATCH Patch - fixes bugs without breaking :This is increased to make corrections to existing data 
that do not alter the schema or add new features. These are data quality improvements. A script 
that worked with version 1.1.1 should produce more accurate results with version 1.1.2. 
The following are specific examples to illustrate each type of change. 
2.1.2. Specific examples of changes

 STRING to accommodate new non-numeric identifiers. 
2. Deleting a column:Remove the last_login_date column because it is considered obsolete or for 
privacy reasons. 
3. Renaming a column:Change the name of the revenue column to gross_revenue_eur for clarity.

5. Modifying units without changing the name: Changing the values in the distance column from 
kilometers to miles would break all dependent calculations.

1. Adding new rows:incorporate the most recent month's sales data into the historical dataset. 
2. Adding a new nullable column:Add a discount_code column that can be empty for past sales. 
3. Data enrichment:Add demographic columns e.g., estimated_age, region from an external data 
source. 
4. Inclusion of a new optional field:In a product JSON, add a new field technical_specifications that 
did not exist before. 
5. Increased granularity:to move from having daily aggregated data to having hourly data, while 
maintaining the column scheme.

in the original formula. 
2. Duplicate removal:Identify and remove 1,000 rows that were exact duplicates. 
3. Correction of typographical errors: Standardize "Madrid", "madrid" and "Madridd" to a single 
value in the city column. 
4. Imputation of zero values:Fill in the NULL values in the height column using the average of your

5. Format standardization: Unify all date formats in the birth_date column to the YYYY -MM-DD 
standard. 
2.1.3. Borderline cases and how to categorize them 
Often, a change may appear to belong to more than one category. The general rule is to choose the 
category with the greatest impact. 
 Scenery:Errors in existing data are corrected PATCH and, in the same batch, new data from last 
week is added MINOR . 
Decision:This change should be versioned as a MINOR. Although it includes patches, the addition 
of new data is a more functionally significant change. The version would change from v1.2.5 to

Decision:This change should be versioned as a MINOR. Although it includes patches, the addition 
of new data is a more functionally significant change. The version would change from v1.2.5 to 
v1.3.0. The commit message or release documentation should detail both actions. 
 Scenery:A column MAJOR is removed, but the column was empty NULL in all records. 
Decision:Although no information is lost in practice, the schema has changed. This is a MAJOR 
change. Any script attempting to select that column will fail. The version will change from v1.3.0 
to v2.0.0. 
2.1.4. Advanced naming conventions 
For more complex workflows, SemVer can be extended with additional tags: 
 Pre-release versions:For experimental or validation data, hyphens and an identifier are added. 
Examples: v2.1.0-alpha, v2.1.0-beta.2. This indicates that the version is not stable and should not 
be used in production. 
 Build metadata: For additional information that does not affect the version, a plus sign is

be used in production. 
 Build metadata: For additional information that does not affect the version, a plus sign is 
added. Example: v3.0.0 timestamp.20250911. Useful for linking a data version to the specific 
pipeline run that generated it. 
2.1.5. Integration with versioning tools 
Semantic versioning is effectively implemented using Git tags. Since tools like DVC link data to code, a 
Git tag captures the state of the code and the pointers to the data simultaneously. An integrated 
workflow might look like this: 
1. Data change:An analyst runs a script that cleans dataset.csv. 
2. Versioned with DVC:The analyst runs dvc add dataset.csv. DVC updates the dataset.csv.dvc file 
with the new dataset hash. 
3. Commitin Git: The analyst adds the changes to the Git staging: git add dataset.csv.dvc and 
commits them with a descriptive message: git commit -m "Fix: Corrects typographical errors in 
the city column".

Technical guide Data version control 18 
 
 
 
4. SemVer's decision:The change is a data correction. It's a patch. If the previous version was v1.1.1, 
the new one will be v1.1.2. 
5. Tagged in Git:An annotated Git tag is created: git tag -a "v1.1.2" -m "Standardized and corrected 
city data." 
6. Synchronization:The changes and the tag are uploaded to the remote repositories: dvc push and 
git push --tags. 
This workflow creates an immutable and clearly labeled reference point that locks a semantic version 
with the exact state of the code and data. 
2.2. Versioning strategies 
Versioning data involves storing multiple snapshots, which can lead to prohibitive storage costs if the 
right strategy isn't chosen. The choice depends on the size of the data, the frequency of changes, and 
access requirements. 
2.2.1. Full versioning Full Snapshotting 
This is the simplest strategy: each time a new version is created, a complete and independent copy of 
the dataset is saved.

access requirements. 
2.2.1. Full versioning Full Snapshotting 
This is the simplest strategy: each time a new version is created, a complete and independent copy of 
the dataset is saved. 
When to use it?: 
 With relatively small datasets up to a few GB . 
 When changes are infrequent but significant e.g., a complete quarterly update . 
 In systems where read speed is the highest priority and storage cost is not a limiting factor. 
2.2.2. Incremental Versioning Delta Versioning 
This strategy focuses on storage efficiency. Only the first complete version is saved; subsequent 
versions are stored as "deltas" or "differentials," which are files that only contain the changes 
 additions, modifications, deletions compared to the previous version. 
Techniques and reconstruction: 
1. Version v1.0:The entire base.csv file is stored. 
2. Version v1.1:The difference between version 1.0 and the new version is calculated. A file

Techniques and reconstruction: 
1. Version v1.0:The entire base.csv file is stored. 
2. Version v1.1:The difference between version 1.0 and the new version is calculated. A file 
named delta_v1.1.diff is generated. Only this small delta file is stored. 
3. Reconstruction:To obtain v1.1, a program must read base.csv and apply the transformations 
described in delta_v1.1.diff. 
To speed up access to the latest version, the full last version and backward deltas can be stored. To 
rebuild an older version, the "reverse" deltas are applied. 
When to use it?: 
 With massive datasets terabytes or more where the cost of storage is the main problem. 
 In systems with frequent and small changes e.g., a database table that is updated every few 
minutes . 
 Data formats such as Delta Lake or Apache Iceberg implement this concept natively.

Technical guide Data version control 19 
 
 
 
2.2.3. Hybrid techniques 
They combine the best of both worlds to balance cost and performance. 
 Implementation:A full snapshot is stored periodically e.g., on every major change or once a week 
and deltas are used for intermediate versions. 
s3: mi-bucket datos v1.0.0 COMPLETE 
s3: my-bucket data deltas v1.0.1.diff 
s3: my-bucket data deltas v1.1.0.diff 
... 
s3: mi-bucket datos v2.0.0 COMPLETE - New starting point 
 
 Applications:This is often the most practical strategy. It limits the length of delta chains, reducing 
the risk of corruption and shortening rebuild times, without completely sacrificing storage 
efficiency. 
2.2.4. Data deduplication and space optimization 
Modern tools like DVC implement an intelligent versioning approach that resembles a hybrid strategy 
with deduplication. DVC uses content-addressable storage. 
1. Hashing:When a file is added dvc add my_file , DVC calculates its checksum hash .

with deduplication. DVC uses content-addressable storage. 
1. Hashing:When a file is added dvc add my_file , DVC calculates its checksum hash . 
2. Central storage:The file is moved to a central storage local or in the cloud , using its hash as the 
filename. 
3. Pointer:In the Git repository, only a small text file .dvc containing the hash of the original file is 
saved. 
If a file remains unchanged in a new version, its hash will be the same, and DVC will simply reuse the 
existing file in central storage. It will not be re-uploaded. If the dataset consists of many files, only the 
new or modified files will be uploaded. This provides automatic file-level deduplication.

2.3. Metadata management and traceability 
A data version without context is just a collection of bits. Metadata provides that context, explaining 
what the data is, where it came from, how it was created, and its quality. Traceability, or lineage, 
connects the versions together, creating a navigable map of the data's evolution. 
2.3.1. What metadata should be captured? 
Capturing the correct metadata is crucial for governance, reproducibility, and automation. A 
comprehensive list is provided below for reference: 
 Provenance metadata: 
 original_source: URL, name of the source database or system.

comprehensive list is provided below for reference: 
 Provenance metadata: 
 original_source: URL, name of the source database or system. 
 data_owner: responsible team or person. 
 version_creation_date: exact timestamp of when the version was generated. 
 user_creator: the ID of the user or service that executed the process. 
 commit_message: human-readable description of the change made. 
 Structural metadata schema : 
 column_list: an array with the names of all the columns. 
 data_types: a map of column name to data type string, int64, float64, datetime . 
 number_of_columns: the total number of columns. 
 restrictions: information about primary keys, unique values, or non-null columns. 
 Descriptive metadata statistics : 
 row_number: the total record count. 
 file_size_bytes: the size of the file on disk. 
 statistics_by_column: for each numeric column: mean, median, standard deviation, 
minimum, maximum.

row_number: the total record count. 
 file_size_bytes: the size of the file on disk. 
 statistics_by_column: for each numeric column: mean, median, standard deviation, 
minimum, maximum. 
 frequency_categories: for each categorical column: count of unique values and frequency of 
the most common ones. 
 percentage_nulls: the percentage of missing values per column. 
 Lineage and reproducibility metadata: 
 version_datos_entrada: the hash or semantic version of the datasets used as input. 
 commit_git_script: the Git commit hash of the transformation script that generated this 
version. 
 script_name: the name of the script or notebook e.g., cleaning.py . 
 execution_parameters: the arguments with which the script was executed e.g. "cut -
off_date": "2025-09-01" . 
 Quality and business metadata: 
 result_of_quality_tests: a summary of the data validations e.g. "passed": 10, "failed": 1 .

 Tags: keywords for search and discovery e.g., "sales", "europe", "PII" . 
 description: a natural language explanation of the purpose of the dataset.

The choice of format depends on human readability and ease of processing by machines. 
 JSON:

structures.

2.3.3. Automation of metadata capture 
Manual metadata capture is prone to errors and does not scale. It should be an automated step within 
the data pipeline. 
Pipeline data with metadata capture 
1. Activator:A git push to the main branch activates a Continuous Integration CI system such as 
Jenkins or GitHub Actions. 
2. Pipeline execution using DVC :The CI executes the dvc repro command, which in turn executes 
the transformation script e.g., process.py . 
3. Metadata generation:The process.py script, in addition to generating the new dataset, uses a 
library e.g., Pandas Profiling to generate a metadata.json file with all the descriptive and schema 
statistics. 
4. Lineage capture:The script also captures CI environment variables such as the Git commit hash 
and adds them to the metadata.json.

statistics. 
4. Lineage capture:The script also captures CI environment variables such as the Git commit hash 
and adds them to the metadata.json. 
5. Metadata versioning: The metadata.json file is versioned along with the data using dvc add 
metadata.json. 
6. Commitand Push:The CI pipeline commits the new .dvc files and metadata.json.dvc, and uploads 
them to the repository. 
2.3.4. Lineage TrackingAutomatic vs. manual 
 Manual:It involves documenting dependencies in a README.md file or a wiki. It's simple but

Technical guide Data version control 22 
 
 
 
fragile, as it easily becomes outdated. 
 Automatic:Tools like DVC formalize the lineage in a pipeline definition file, typically dvc.yaml: 
stages: process_data: cmd: python scripts process.py data raw.csv data processed.csv 
deps: - data raw.csv - scripts process.py outs: - data processed.csv 
 
This file explicitly declares that processed.csv an output, out depends on raw.csv and process.py 
 dependencies, deps . This creates a Directed Acyclic Graph DAG that DVC can understand. If 
raw.csv or process.py changes, DVC knows that processed.csv is outdated and needs to be 
regenerated. 
2.4. Branchingand merging for datasets 
Branching is one of Git's most powerful features, as it allows for parallel work and isolated 
experimentation. Applying this concept to data is possible, but merging is conceptually very different 
and more complex than with code. 
2.4.1. When should you create branches in data?

experimentation. Applying this concept to data is possible, but merging is conceptually very different 
and more complex than with code. 
2.4.1. When should you create branches in data? 
1. Safe experimentation:A data scientist wants to test a new null imputation technique. They create 
a branch called feature null -imputation, make the changes, and generate a new version of the 
data only in that branch. The production dataset in the main branch remains untouched. 
2. Development of new features: A team is integrating a new data source. They create a develop 
branch where they work on merging and cleaning the new dataset. Only when the data is 
validated and stable is it merged into main. 
3. Bug Fixes Hotfix :A serious quality issue was discovered in the production data version v3.1.4. A 
hotfix price-correction branch was created from the v3.1.4 tag, the problem was fixed, v3.1.5 was 
generated, and it was merged back into main. 
2.4.2. Merge strategies for datasets

hotfix price-correction branch was created from the v3.1.4 tag, the problem was fixed, v3.1.5 was 
generated, and it was merged back into main. 
2.4.2. Merge strategies for datasets 
Directly merging large binary or CSV data files as with git merge is not feasible. Instead, higher -level 
strategies are used. 
Strategy 1: Code merging and regeneration most common and safe 
This is the preferred approach. The conflict is not resolved in the data itself, but in the code that 
generates it. The flow would be as follows: 
1. Divergence:Ana creates the branch feature eliminar -nulos and modifies clean.py. Bruno 
creates feature imputar-nulos and also modifies clean.py. 
2. Code merging:Ana's branch is merged into main first. Bruno now has to merge the changes 
from main into his branch. Git will detect a conflict in the clean.py file. 
3. Code resolution:Bruno resolves the conflict in the Python script, deciding which cleanup logic 
to use or a combination .

3. Code resolution:Bruno resolves the conflict in the Python script, deciding which cleanup logic 
to use or a combination . 
4. Regeneration:Once the clean.py code is merged and there are no conflicts in main, the entire 
pipeline dvc repro is executed. This script regenerates the final dataset from the original

Technical guide Data version control 23 
 
 
 
sources, creating a new version of the data that reflects the merged code logic. 
Strategy 2: Merging at the record level For specific cases 
When two branches modify different and non-overlapping subsets of data. 
 Use case:The European team works on the feature data -europe branch and adds clients from 
Spain. The LATAM team works on feature data-latam and adds clients from Mexico. 
 Resolution:The "merge" can be a script that performs a UNION or concatenation operation on the 
two resulting datasets to create the final version. 
2.4.3. Data conflict resolution 
A real data conflict occurs when two branches modify the same record with different values. 
 Scenery:In branch A, the price of product_id 123 is corrected to 50.0. In branch B, it is corrected 
to 51.5. 
 Resolution:This cannot be resolved automatically. It requires manual intervention or a predefined 
policy.

to 51.5. 
 Resolution:This cannot be resolved automatically. It requires manual intervention or a predefined 
policy. 
 Manual intervention:A data steward or data owner must be notified to decide what the 
correct value is. 
 Resolution policy:Business rules can be established, such as "always prioritize the value of 
the pricing system over manual adjustment" or "the most recent change takes priority". 
2.5. Retention and archiving policies 
It is neither practical nor cost-effective to keep every version of every dataset permanently accessible 
in high-performance storage. Clear policies are needed to decide what to store, for how long, and 
where. 
2.5.1. Criteria for deciding which versions to keep 
 Production versions: Any version of data used to train a production model or to generate a 
regulatory report must be retained indefinitely or as required by law. These versions must be 
clearly labeled v1.2.0, report_Q3_2024 .

regulatory report must be retained indefinitely or as required by law. These versions must be 
clearly labeled v1.2.0, report_Q3_2024 . 
 Milestone versions:preserve versions that represent important milestones of the project, such as 
the end of a research phase or data used for a scientific publication. 
 Recent versions:Keep all versions from the last N months e.g., 6 months in "hot" storage to 
facilitate rollback and quick comparison. 
 Intermediate versions:Versions generated in branches of experimental features that were not 
merged may be deleted after a grace period e.g., 30 days . 
Regulatory issues would need to be taken into account in these types of decisions, such as: 
 GDPR:It requires that personal data not be kept longer than necessary. Retention policies must 
reflect this, ensuring the deletion of old data. Data lineage helps demonstrate that a user's data 
has been removed from all derived versions.

reflect this, ensuring the deletion of old data. Data lineage helps demonstrate that a user's data 
has been removed from all derived versions. 
 SOX Finance , HIPAA Health : They require strict traceability and the ability to audit data over 
long periods often 7 years or more .

Technical guide Data version control 24 
 
 
 
2.5.2. Long-term archiving strategies 
Archiving involves moving old and infrequently accessed data to low -cost, high-latency storage. One 
possible archiving policy could be: 
1. Monthly evaluation:An automated script runs on the first day of each month. 
2. Candidate identification:The script lists all data versions that are older than six months and 
do not have a "milestone" or "production" label. 
3. Move to cold storage:Using the cloud provider's API, the script changes the storage class of 
the data files from S3 Standard to S3 Glacier Deep Archive AWS example . 
4. Metadata update: A metadata record is updated to indicate that version v0.5.3 is now 
archived and its retrieval could take several hours. 
5. Elimination optional :For very old versions that exceed the legal retention period, the script 
can permanently delete them.

3. VERSIONING TOOLS AND TECHNIQUES IN PRACTICE 
After establishing the conceptual and strategic foundations of Data Version Control DVC , this section 
moves from theory to practice, exploring the most influential tools and applied techniques that enable 
data teams to implement robust, reproducible, and collaborative workflows. The goal is to provide a 
detailed and functional technical guide that serves as a field manual for any professional facing the 
challenge of managing the data lifecycle.

3.1. DVC Data Version Control 
DVC is an open-source tool that brings Git principles to the world of data and machine learning models. 
It's not a replacement for Git; it's a layer that works seamlessly with it. While Git manages the code and

It's not a replacement for Git; it's a layer that works seamlessly with it. While Git manages the code and 
metadata small text files , DVC handles the larger files data, models, etc. , maintaining the connection 
between them. This synergy allows for the creation of fully reproducible data science projects.

3.1.3. All basic commands 
Here are examples of the fundamental DVC workflow in daily use, executed from a Windows terminal. 
Step 1. dvc add: start versioning a data file 
This command tells DVC to start scanning a file or directory. 
 Example:Let's assume we have a data file data clientes.csv. 
 
What has dvc add done?: 
1. It has moved the contents of data clientes.csv to the internal DVC cache .dvc cache . 
2. It has created a small metadata file called data clientes.csv.dvc. This file is a plain text pointer that 
contains the MD5 hash of the original file's contents. 
3. He modified the .gitignore file to ensure that Git ignores data clientes.csv and does not upload it 
to the remote code repository. 
Step 2. git commit: save the data state 
The next step is to save the pointer file .dvc in Git. This is how a version of the code is associated with 
a version of the data.

Step 2. git commit: save the data state 
The next step is to save the pointer file .dvc in Git. This is how a version of the code is associated with 
a version of the data. 
 
Now, anyone who clones the repository and sees this commit will know which version of the data was 
being used. 
Step 3. dvc push: Upload the data to remote storage 
 Create a folder for the data and a sample file 
mkdir dataecho "id,nombre,ciudad" data clientes.csvecho "1,Ana,Madrid" 
data clientes.csvecho "2,Bruno,Barcelona" data clientes.csv 
 
 Add the file to DVC version control 
dvc add data clientes.csv 
 Check the status of the Git repository 
git status 
 
 Add the .dvc file and the modified .gitignore 
git adddata clientes.csv.dvc.gitignore 
 
 Confirm the changes 
git commit -m "Add raw client dataset"

Until now, the data is only in your local cache. dvc push uploads it to the configured remote storage. 
This command reads the .dvc files from the current commit, finds the corresponding data in the local 
cache, and uploads them to remote storage. 
Step 4. dvc pull: Download the data from remote storage 
This is the command that a collaborator or you yourself on another machine would use to obtain 
the data. 
 Contributor workflow: 
1. Clone the Git repository: git clone repo_url 
2. Enter the directory: cd project-dvc-guide 
3. Execute dvc pull 
dvc pull reads the .dvc files from the current commit, checks if the data is in the local cache and, if not, 
downloads it from remote storage, placing it in the correct path e.g., data clientes.csv .

3.1.5. Pipelinesdata with DVC 
This is where DVC shines. It allows you to define, execute, and version the steps in a data pipeline, 
creating an explicit lineage. Pipelines are defined in the dvc.yaml file. 
Key concept:A pipeline is composed of stages. Each stage has: 
 cmd: a command to execute. 
 deps: dependencies files or directories that are inputs . 
 outs: outputs files or directories that are outputs .

If a dependency changes, DVC knows that the stage is "obsolete" and needs to be re-executed. 
Example of a simple pipeline: preprocessing 
Suppose we have a Python script src preprocess.py that takes data clientes.csv and generates 
data clientes_procesado.parquet. 
The stage is created with dvc stage add. This command creates or updates the dvc.yaml file. 
The generated dvc.yaml file will look like this: 
To run the pipeline, simply execute the command dvc repro . This command examines the dvc.yaml 
file, checks if any dependencies have changed since the last execution, and if so, re -executes the 
outdated stages. If nothing has changed, it will do nothing.

3.1.7. Troubleshootingof common problems 
Some common problems and their possible solutions are: 
1. Problem:ERROR: failed to push data to the cloud - The specified bucket does not exist 
 Symptom:dvc push fails with an authentication or resource not found error.

1. Problem:ERROR: failed to push data to the cloud - The specified bucket does not exist 
 Symptom:dvc push fails with an authentication or resource not found error. 
 Probable cause: The cloud credentials are not configured in the environment, or the 
bucket container name in .dvc config is incorrect. 
 Solution:Verify that your CLI AWS, gcloud, az is correctly configured and authenticated. 
Check the .dvc config file to ensure the remote URL is correct. 
2. Problem:dvc repro does nothing, even though I've changed the data. 
 Symptom:You run dvc repro but DVC says that everything is up to date. 
 Probable cause:You haven't used dvc add to register the change in the data. DVC only detects 
changes in the files it crawls. 
 Solution:run dvc add modified_file before dvc repro. 
3. Problem:I accidentally added a large file to Git. 
 Symptom:git push takes forever or is rejected by the server due to file size.

Solution:run dvc add modified_file before dvc repro. 
3. Problem:I accidentally added a large file to Git. 
 Symptom:git push takes forever or is rejected by the server due to file size. 
 Probable cause:You forgot to add the file to .gitignore before doing git add .. 
 Solution:Use tools like git -filter-repo or BFG Repo -Cleaner to remove the file from the Git 
history. Then, add it back with dvc add and commit the .dvc file. 
4. Problem:ERROR: file has no checksum 
 Symptom:dvc pull fails because it cannot find a hash in the cache or on the remote. 
 Probable cause:The .dvc file you are trying to download was created incorrectly, or someone 
did dvc push without doing dvc add first. 
 Solution:Go back to the original commit where the data was added and make sure the dvc 
add - git commit - dvc push flow was executed correctly. You may need to regenerate the 
output and force a new push. 
5. Problem:DVC performance is slow with thousands of small files.

add - git commit - dvc push flow was executed correctly. You may need to regenerate the 
output and force a new push. 
5. Problem:DVC performance is slow with thousands of small files. 
 Symptom:Commands like dvc status or dvc push take a long time.

Technical guide Data version control 32 
 
 
 
 Probable cause:DVC calculates a hash for each file. With many small files, the overhead is 
considerable. 
 Solution:instead of dvc add data images which tracks each image individually , groups the 
files into a single .zip or .tar file and versions that single file: dvc adddata images.zip. 
6. Problem:Cache directory is growing too large 
 Symptom:the directory.dvc cacheIt takes up a lot of disk space. 
 Probable cause:The cache contains data versions of old branches or discarded experiments. 
 Solution:Use dvc gc to clear the cache. Make sure you have a backup if you want to keep 
data from branches that aren't in your local repository. 
3.2. Git LFS - An alternative for large files 
Git LFS Large File Storage is a Git extension developed by GitHub for handling large files. To use it on 
Windows, you must first install Git for Windows, and then rungit lfs installonly once.

Git LFS Large File Storage is a Git extension developed by GitHub for handling large files. To use it on 
Windows, you must first install Git for Windows, and then rungit lfs installonly once. 
Like DVC, it doesn't store large files directly in the Git repository. Instead, it saves a small text file with 
a pointer, and the large file is uploaded to a separate storage area. 
The main difference with DVC is philosophical and functional: 
 Git LFSIt is a Git extension, designed to make versioning large files almost transparent. 
 DVCIt is a data science tool, designed to understand and manage pipelines and experiments, 
that uses Git as part of its workflow.

3.3. Native cloud solutions 
Major cloud providers offer native versioning capabilities in their object storage services. These 
powerful tools can be a viable alternative, especially if you're already deeply embedded in a particular

powerful tools can be a viable alternative, especially if you're already deeply embedded in a particular 
cloud ecosystem. Unlike DVC or Git LFS, they don't integrate directly with Git; instead, they operate at 
the bucket or container level. 
3.3.1. AWS S3 with native versioning 
 Description:By enabling versioning on an S3 bucket, whenever an object is overwritten or deleted, 
S3 doesn't delete it, but instead creates a new version and preserves the old one. Each version 
has a unique ID. Any previous version can be listed, restored, or permanently deleted. 
 Key functionality:Object Lock and retention for compliance. 
3.3.2. Google Cloud Storage GCS versioning 
 Description:Similar to S3, GCS object versioning, when enabled on a bucket, saves an "outdated" 
version whenever an object is replaced or deleted. These older versions can be listed and 
managed. 
 Key functionality:lifecycle policies to automatically remove or change the storage class of older

managed. 
 Key functionality:lifecycle policies to automatically remove or change the storage class of older 
versions e.g., move versions older than 90 days to Coldline Storage .

Technical guide Data version control 34 
 
 
 
3.3.3. Azure Blob Storage options 
 Description:Azure offers two main mechanisms: 
1. Versioning:It automatically creates new versions of a blob every time it is modified. It is the 
option most similar to S3 and GCS. 
2. Snapshots:These are read -only snapshots of a blob at a given moment, which must be 
created manually. They are useful for point backups. 
 Key functionality:immutability at the account or container level for compliance requirements.

These native versioning solutions integrate seamlessly with the same vendor's ML platforms. For

training run with a specific dataset version ID.

datasets.

The main disadvantage is that lineage the connection between code and data version must be 
managed manually or with the help of ML platform tools, which is often less explicit and portable than 
the solution offered by DVC. 
3.4. Manual versioning and conventions 
Not all projects need the power and overhead of a tool like DVC. For small, single-researcher projects, 
or in very restrictive environments, a disciplined manual versioning system may suffice. The key is 
consistency. 
3.4.1. When is manual versioning appropriate? 
 Solo projects:when there is no need to collaborate or resolve conflicts. 
 Datasetssmall and stable:if the data changes infrequently and its size is manageable. 
 Initial exploration phases:during rapid prototyping, where the formality of a CVD system can slow 
down the process.

Initial exploration phases:during rapid prototyping, where the formality of a CVD system can slow 
down the process. 
 Restrictive corporate environments:where it is not possible to install open source tools like DVC. 
3.4.2. Robust naming conventions 
A good naming convention is the cornerstone of manual version control. It should be informative and 
easy to organize. A logical structure might be: 
 DATASET_NAME _ ISO_DATE _ SEMVER_VERSION _ STATE .ext 
 DATASET_NAME : descriptive base name e.g., sales_customers . 
 ISO_DATE YYYYMMDD or YYYY-MM-DD. Allows you to sort files chronologically. 
 VERSION_SEMVER : vMAJOR.MINOR.PATCH e.g., v1.2.0 . Follows the logic of semantic 
versioning. 
 STATE : optional, but useful e.g. raw, cleaned, final, dev . 
 .ext: file extension e.g., .csv, .parquet . 
Examples: 
 sales_historical_20250915_v1.0.0_raw.csv Initial raw data 
 sales_historical_20250916_v1.1.0_cleaned.parquet Cleaned data with new columns

.ext: file extension e.g., .csv, .parquet . 
Examples: 
 sales_historical_20250915_v1.0.0_raw.csv Initial raw data 
 sales_historical_20250916_v1.1.0_cleaned.parquet Cleaned data with new columns 
 clients_segmentation_20250917_v2.0.0_final.csv Final version with changes to the schema 
3.4.3. Templatesfor manual documentation 
Along with the versioned data, there should be a text file ideally Markdown that documents the 
changes.

4.5. Conclusion 
This case study completes the basic data versioning cycle with DVC in a local, controlled environment. 
It demonstrates how to set up a project from scratch, systematically version datasets, update data 
while maintaining a complete history, and revert changes when necessary. This minimalist, yet simple, 
workflow encapsulates the fundamental principles of data version control developed throughout this 
guide. 
The key to success in implementing a data versioning system lies not only in the choice of tool or 
technical configuration, but also in the discipline and best practices adopted by the team. As discussed 
in previous sections, this includes:

 Automatic capture of metadata that provides context and traceability. 
 Seamless integration between Git and DVC to keep code and data synchronized.

Technical guide Data version control 44 
 
 
 
By consistently following these practices, any organization, regardless of size or complexity, can 
establish a robust, auditable, and reproducible data management system that will serve as a solid 
foundation for advanced analytics, machine learning, and scientific research projects. 
5. BEST PRACTICES AND IMPLEMENTATION 
The successful implementation of a data version control system depends not only on the choice of 
technical tools but also on the adoption of consistent and sustainable organizational practices. This 
final section summarizes the essential governance recommendations, phased adoption processes, and 
fundamental resources that will facilitate the transition from informal systems to professional data 
lifecycle management. The goal is to provide a practical and scalable framework that teams can 
implement gradually , starting with basic conventions and evolving toward more sophisticated

lifecycle management. The goal is to provide a practical and scalable framework that teams can 
implement gradually , starting with basic conventions and evolving toward more sophisticated 
processes as their specific needs evolve. 
5.1. Fundamental conventions and standards 
The first step toward a robust versioning system is to establish clear naming and documentation 
conventions. These conventions should be simple enough to avoid friction in daily work, yet rigorous 
enough to ensure traceability. For version names, it is recommended to adopt data-adapted semantic 
versioning MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH , where each component clearly communicates the nature of the 
change. File names should follow predictable patterns that include the dataset name, the date in ISO 
format YYYY-MM-DD , and the semantic version. 
Commit messages associated with data changes should follow a consistent structure that includes: the

format YYYY-MM-DD , and the semantic version. 
Commit messages associated with data changes should follow a consistent structure that includes: the 
type of change add, modify, correct , a concise description of what was modified, and the justification 
for the change. For example: "PATCH: Corrected nul l values in temperature column - 15 records with 
impossible values -100 C were detected and imputed using the 24-hour moving average." 
This structure greatly facilitates subsequent auditing and understanding of the change history. 
Supporting documentation should be maintained in a CHANGELOG.md file that records each released 
version with its date, changes made, and potential impact on existing analyses. 
5.2. Governance roles and processes 
Effective governance requires clearly defined responsibilities, even in small teams. It is recommended 
to establish at least three fundamental roles: 
 Data Owner, responsible for strategic decisions regarding the dataset and its evolution.

to establish at least three fundamental roles: 
 Data Owner, responsible for strategic decisions regarding the dataset and its evolution. 
 Data Steward , responsible for the operational management of versioning and quality 
assurance. 
 Data Consumers, users who consume the published versions for their analysis. 
In small teams, one person can assume multiple roles, but the conceptual distinction helps to clarify 
responsibilities.

Technical guide Data version control 45 
 
 
 
Review and approval processes should be adapted to the type of change. Patch changes minor fixes 
may only require a technical quality review, while major changes those that break compatibility must 
undergo a more thorough review process that includes a ssessing the impact on existing pipelines and 
models, as well as providing advance notice to all affected customers. It is recommended to establish 
a simplified emergency procedure for critical situations where serious errors need to be corrected 
immediately, always followed by a post-mortem review. 
5.3. Compliance and retention considerations 
Version retention policies must balance regulatory requirements with storage costs. As a general rule, 
all versions used for production models or regulatory reports should be retained indefinitely or as 
required by applicable regulations typically 7 10 ye ars in the financial and healthcare sectors .

required by applicable regulations typically 7 10 ye ars in the financial and healthcare sectors . 
Development and experimental versions can be archived or deleted after a reasonable grace period 
 30 90 days . For organizations subject to personal data processing regulations, it is critical to establish 
procedures that document how personal data is managed through versioning, including the ability to 
remove information about specific individuals from all versions when the right to be forgotten is 
exercised.

5.4. Roadmapprogressive implementation 
Adopting a data version control system should be gradual and pragmatic. A phased approach is 
recommended to allow for rapid value creation and learning from each stage before scaling. 
Phase 1 Weeks 1-4 :Foundation. Select a critical dataset as a pilot, establish basic naming conventions, 
implement DVC with local or simple cloud storage, and train the team on essential commands add, 
commit, push, pull, checkout . The goal is to achieve the first versioned version and for the team to 
become familiar with the basic workflow.

Technical guide Data version control 46 
 
 
 
Phase 2 Weeks 5-12 :Expansion. Extend versioning to the 3-5 most critical datasets, implement basic 
DVC pipelines to capture data lineage, establish simple review procedures, and create documentation 
templates. This phase consolidates practices and begins to generate tangible benefits in reproducibility. 
Phase 3 Weeks 13-24 :Maturity. Implement branching for experiments, automate metadata capture 
using scripts, integrate versioning with CI CD pipelines, and establish formal retention and archiving 
policies. At this stage, the system is fully operational and the team has mastered advanced workflows. 
Phase 4 Months 6 : Optimization. Evaluate usage and efficiency metrics, optimize storage costs 
through hybrid strategies, scale the system across the entire organization, and implement automated 
monitoring and alerts. This phase focuses on continuous improvement and enterprise scalability. 
5.5. Tool decision matrix

monitoring and alerts. This phase focuses on continuous improvement and enterprise scalability. 
5.5. Tool decision matrix 
Choosing the right tool depends on multiple contextual factors: 
 For small teams 1 -5 people working with datasets up to 10 GB, a manual versioning system 
with strict naming conventions may be sufficient. 
 For medium-sized teams 5 -20 people with datasets up to 100 GB and active collaboration 
needs, DVC is the recommended option due to its balance between power and simplicity. 
 For large organizations with massive datasets 100 GB and enterprise requirements, cloud -
native solutions S3 Versioning, GCS, Azure Blob integrated with ML platforms may be more 
appropriate. Git LFS is a viable alternative for small datasets 5 GB when the team is already 
very familiar with Git and prefers a minimal learning curve.
